nativeRange.habitat: Submerged in slow-moving to still freshwater bodies — rivers, lakes, ponds, and backwaters — typically on soft sediment substrates where deep water and adequate light penetration allow leaves to reach the surface. Often found in deeper water than smaller congeners due to its ribbon-leaf length.

decorPreferences: Tall tank with at least 60 cm of water column so adult leaves can stand vertically before bending at the surface
Fine sand or fine gravel substrate at least 5 cm deep to anchor the bulbiform rhizome and accommodate aggressive runners
Open background area — the only correct placement; this plant will quickly dominate any midground or foreground space
Root tabs near the crown to feed the heavy-feeding bulbous root system
Keep the crown at or barely above the substrate line — burying the rhizome causes crown rot

goodWith: Large peaceful display fish (silver dollars only in very tall tanks where leaves stay above browse height)
avoidWith: Goldfish (will shred or uproot ribbon leaves)
Large digging cichlids (oscars, large central american cichlids)
Plant-eating fish (silver dollars in shorter tanks, large plecos, large barbs)
Copper-based medications (Vallisneria is sensitive to copper)
Liquid carbon / glutaraldehyde supplements (Excel-type products commonly cause Vallisneria melt)

summary: Giant Val (Vallisneria gigantea) is the largest Vallisneria in the aquarium trade, with ribbon leaves that routinely exceed 1 m and can reach 1.5–2 m in deep tanks. The trade name covers a taxonomically contested group that includes V. australis. Suited only to very tall display tanks.
careNotes:
Giant Val is the largest Vallisneria in the aquarium trade. Mature ribbon leaves routinely exceed 1 m and frequently reach 1.5–2 m — far beyond the proportions of V. americana (60–120 cm), V. spiralis (30–60 cm), or V. asiatica. In the aquarium trade, the name Vallisneria gigantea is applied consistently to the very large Indo-Pacific and Australian form regardless of which accepted scientific name applies to any given population, and the plant is sold and kept under that trade name worldwide.

Husbandry follows the same principles as other Vallisneria species, with one critical exception: DO NOT BURY THE RHIZOME. The bulbiform crown (the swollen base from which the leaves emerge) must sit at or barely above the substrate surface — burying it causes crown rot. Fine sand or fine gravel at least 5 cm deep provides the best anchoring. Because Giant Val feeds heavily through its root system, root tabs placed near the crown are generally recommended to sustain long-term growth.

Once established, the plant spreads aggressively by stolons — horizontal runners that produce daughter plants along their length — though it tends to propagate somewhat more slowly than V. spiralis. Low-to-medium lighting is tolerated, with medium lighting preferred for the most vigorous growth. CO2 supplementation is optional but tends to improve leaf quality and growth rate.

Two common aquarium products are known to damage or kill Vallisneria: copper-based fish medications and liquid-carbon supplements (such as glutaraldehyde-based products sold under names like Excel). Both can trigger rapid leaf melt, so they should not be used in any tank where Vallisneria is present.

Although many hobbyist resources rate Vallisneria as easy to keep, Giant Val is more practically suited to intermediate keepers, largely because of its height requirement. The species cannot thrive in a tank shallower than roughly 60 cm; in shorter aquariums, the long leaves bend at the surface and eventually rot. For a tall background plant in a standard 45 cm aquarium, V. americana ([fw-plant-034]) is the more appropriate choice. Giant Val is best reserved for genuine display or show tanks with 60 cm or more of water depth.

primary.name: Aquarium Breeder — Vallisneria Care Guide
primary.notes: Most usable primary care source: explicitly distinguishes Giant Val from V. spiralis and V. americana, confirms leaves 'up to 1 m or more', identifies bulbiform rhizome structure, notes Giant Val 'grows fast but propagates slower than other Vallisneria species' and 'prefers slightly higher temperatures'. Genus-level water parameters used: 20–28°C, pH 6.5–8.0, gH 4–18.

additional.name: BucePlant — Vallisneria Gigantea
additional.notes: Vendor care sheet: family Hydrocharitaceae, common name Giant Val, lighting moderate-to-high, CO2 recommended, growth rate fast, propagation via cut lateral shoots (runners), pH 6–7.5, heavy root feeder requiring quality substrate, background placement, melt risk with drastic parameter swings. Vendor-listed size (12–20 in) is inconsistent with all other sources and was disregarded in favor of Aquarium Breeder and Wikipedia.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Vallisneria (genus)
additional.notes: Taxonomic anchor: 'The largest varieties are often called Vallisneria gigantea regardless of their actual taxonomic designation; most of the plants sold as Vallisneria gigantea are actually Vallisneria americana.' Confirms giant-form leaves 'frequently exceed 1 m in length' and the form is 'only suitable for very large tanks'. Family Hydrocharitaceae.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database — Vallisneria gigantea
additional.notes: Treats V. gigantea as a synonym of V. gracilis and a misapplied name for V. australis (southern Australia, temperate to subtropical). Used to support the Indo-Pacific/Australian native-range framing and the 'taxonomically contested' synthesis.

additional.name: Flowgrow Aquatic Plant Database — Vallisneria australis
additional.notes: Care-parameter cross-check for the V. australis treatment of giant trade Val: optimal 18–28°C (tolerates 4–32°C), pH 6–8, KH 2–21°dKH, fast growth via runners, difficulty 'easy', native to southern Australia. Cannot grow emersed.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Vallisneria spiralis
additional.notes: Confirms historical 'V. s. gigantea' varietal naming and that V. spiralis itself only reaches ~1 m leaves with ~0.75 in width — distinct from the larger giant-form covered here. Family Hydrocharitaceae confirmed.
